items.q: What does an NMR chemical shift (δ, in ppm) actually measure?
items.correct: The position of a peak RELATIVE to TMS (tetramethylsilane, δ = 0), normalised by the spectrometer frequency.
items.misconception: Chemical shift is the absolute resonance frequency of the nucleus in Hz.
items.random_wrong: Chemical shift is the mass of the molecule divided by 1000.
items.why: Students see ppm and think it's an absolute scale, not realising it's a frequency-independent ratio against a reference.

items.q: Why does increasing temperature increase the rate of reaction significantly?
items.correct: Mainly because a much larger fraction of collisions have energy at least equal to the activation energy; collision frequency increases only slightly.
items.misconception: Particles collide much more frequently at higher temperature, which is the main reason for the rate increase.
items.random_wrong: Higher temperature increases the concentration of the reactants.
items.why: Students focus on the more intuitive 'faster movement = more collisions' and overlook that the exponential effect comes from the energy distribution shifting past Ea.

items.q: What distinguishes a heterogeneous from a homogeneous catalyst?
items.correct: A heterogeneous catalyst is in a different phase from the reactants; a homogeneous catalyst is in the same phase.
items.misconception: Heterogeneous catalysts are solid and homogeneous catalysts are liquid — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Heterogeneous catalysts are consumed; homogeneous catalysts are not.
items.why: Students learn 'solid catalyst' examples (iron in Haber) and over-generalise — the actual distinction is about phase relative to the reactants, not the catalyst's physical state alone.
The answer examiners want is: Gillespie and Nyholm 1957. “Lewis 1916” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Lewis dot structures were proposed in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1916. “1869” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 109.5 degrees. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
